fr:mainmatter.html:p: An optimization where two address spaces share the same physical page until one writes to it. Both processes have PTEs pointing at the same PPN, but with the write bit cleared. The first write traps to the OS, which allocates a fresh page, copies the contents, points the writing process's PTE at the new page (with writes re-enabled), and resumes.
Used pervasively:

html:ul.html:li: fork() — the child shares the parent's pages until either modifies one. Avoids the cost of copying an entire address space the child often immediately overwrites with exec().
Shared libraries — many processes map the same library text pages.
Kernel mapped into every process — same physical kernel code seen from every address space.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Once swap space is in play, the OS must decide which pages to evict from physical memory. The mechanism is the Page fault machinery; the question of policy is what this hub covers.

Physical memory is finite, so the OS reserves a region of disk — swap space — to hold pages that don't fit. Pages currently on disk have their present bit cleared in the Page table entry; the PTE instead holds a disk address.
When a process touches a virtual address whose page is not present, the hardware traps to the OS. This is a page fault. The handler:

html:ol.html:li: Looks at the PTE: if invalid, the access is illegal — terminate the process.
If valid but not present: read the page in from swap, update the PTE, retry the access.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The hardware never handles page faults itself — that would require it to understand disk I/O and the OS's swap layout. Page-fault handling is always in software.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The three cases on every memory access:

html:ol.html:li: Present + valid: hardware reads the PPN from the PTE and proceeds.
Not present + valid: hardware invokes the page-fault handler to retrieve from swap.
Not valid: hardware raises an exception; the OS typically kills the process.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. What happens when you dereference a nullptr? The TLB misses on VPN 0, the page-table walk finds the PTE marked invalid, and the hardware traps to the OS — which delivers SIGSEGV.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Least recently used is the standard heuristic for picking a page to evict: throw out the one that has been untouched the longest. It works because of the principle of locality — recently-used pages are likely to be used again soon (temporal locality).
Pure LRU requires updating a data structure on every memory access, which is too expensive. The clock algorithm approximates it cheaply:

html:ol.html:li: Maintain a reference bit per page, set by hardware on access.
Loop a pointer around the pages. If the reference bit is 1, clear it and advance.
If the reference bit is 0, evict that page.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. LRU is not optimal. The pathological case is the looping workload: if the working set is one page larger than the cache, LRU misses 100% of the time.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The quality of any replacement policy is measured by its average memory access time:
where T_P is physical-memory access time and T_D is disk access time. The disk gap is so large that even tiny miss rates dominate AMAT.
A desirable property of a page-replacement policy is the stack property: increasing the cache size never makes the miss rate worse. Surprisingly, not every reasonable-looking policy has it.

FIFO eviction violates the stack property — on certain access sequences, growing the cache strictly increases the number of misses. This is Belady's anomaly. It is what motivates LRU and the clock algorithm-style policies, which do have the stack property because they only depend on relative access recency.

The optimal eviction policy (offline) is to evict the page whose next use is farthest in the future. Unimplementable in practice (you don't know the future), but useful as a theoretical upper bound to compare against.

Two refinements make eviction practical on real workloads. A pure global policy lets one greedy process hog memory, which Segmented FIFO fixes with a per-process resident-set cap; and writing victims back one page at a time wastes disk bandwidth, which Page clustering amortizes by batching the dirty list.

A process-aware refinement of LRU and the clock algorithm. Pure FIFO is a global policy — it does not distinguish memory by owner, so one greedy process can evict everyone else's pages. Segmented FIFO instead maintains a per-process FIFO queue capped at that process's resident set size, bounding how much physical memory any single process can hold.
Evicted pages are not discarded outright; they move to one of two secondary lists:

html:ol.html:li: Clean page free list — evicted pages that were never modified.
Dirty page list — evicted pages that must be written back before reuse.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The secondary lists act as a victim cache: if a process faults on a page still sitting on the dirty list, it can be reclaimed without going to disk. The resident-set cap gives fairness; the secondary lists soften the cost of capping it too aggressively.

An optimization where two address spaces share the same physical page until one writes to it. Both processes have PTEs pointing at the same PPN, but with the write bit cleared. The first write traps to the OS, which allocates a fresh page, copies the contents, points the writing process's PTE at the new page (with writes re-enabled), and resumes.
Used pervasively:

html:ul.html:li: fork() — the child shares the parent's pages until either modifies one. Avoids the cost of copying an entire address space the child often immediately overwrites with exec().
Shared libraries — many processes map the same library text pages.
Kernel mapped into every process — same physical kernel code seen from every address space.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When a process asks for new heap memory, the OS must guarantee the returned pages contain zeros (to avoid leaking other processes' data). The naive implementation zeroes the page at allocation time. On-demand zeroing defers that work to first touch: until the process actually accesses a freshly-allocated page, no zero-fill happens.

The OS marks the page as not-present in the Page table. The first access traps as a Page fault, the handler zeroes a physical page, installs it in the PTE, and resumes. Heap memory that is allocated but never used costs nothing.

An amortization trick for the write side of page replacement. Writing one dirty page to swap at a time pays a full disk I/O per eviction, and small random writes are the worst case for a disk. Page clustering batches them: the OS collects a group of dirty pages from the global dirty list and writes them out together in a single larger, more sequential I/O. The per-page cost of paging out falls because the fixed overhead of a disk write is spread across the whole batch.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A common convention is to map the kernel into every process's address space (via Copy-on-write) so kernel code and structures appear at the same virtual addresses everywhere.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The Unix system call that duplicates the calling Process. After fork() returns, two processes exist where there was one. The child gets its own copy of:

html:ol.html:li: Address space.
General-purpose registers.
Program counter — the child returns from fork() at the same instruction the parent does.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The two processes are distinguished by fork()'s return value:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Which process is scheduled first after fork() is non-deterministic. Use wait() if you need ordering.
Remark. The copy is logical, not physical: real implementations use Copy-on-write so the duplicated address space only allocates fresh pages when one side writes to them.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Once swap space is in play, the OS must decide which pages to evict from physical memory. The mechanism is the Page fault machinery; the question of policy is what this hub covers.

Physical memory is finite, so the OS reserves a region of disk — swap space — to hold pages that don't fit. Pages currently on disk have their present bit cleared in the Page table entry; the PTE instead holds a disk address.
When a process touches a virtual address whose page is not present, the hardware traps to the OS. This is a page fault. The handler:

html:ol.html:li: Looks at the PTE: if invalid, the access is illegal — terminate the process.
If valid but not present: read the page in from swap, update the PTE, retry the access.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The hardware never handles page faults itself — that would require it to understand disk I/O and the OS's swap layout. Page-fault handling is always in software.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The three cases on every memory access:

html:ol.html:li: Present + valid: hardware reads the PPN from the PTE and proceeds.
Not present + valid: hardware invokes the page-fault handler to retrieve from swap.
Not valid: hardware raises an exception; the OS typically kills the process.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. What happens when you dereference a nullptr? The TLB misses on VPN 0, the page-table walk finds the PTE marked invalid, and the hardware traps to the OS — which delivers SIGSEGV.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Least recently used is the standard heuristic for picking a page to evict: throw out the one that has been untouched the longest. It works because of the principle of locality — recently-used pages are likely to be used again soon (temporal locality).
Pure LRU requires updating a data structure on every memory access, which is too expensive. The clock algorithm approximates it cheaply:

html:ol.html:li: Maintain a reference bit per page, set by hardware on access.
Loop a pointer around the pages. If the reference bit is 1, clear it and advance.
If the reference bit is 0, evict that page.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. LRU is not optimal. The pathological case is the looping workload: if the working set is one page larger than the cache, LRU misses 100% of the time.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The quality of any replacement policy is measured by its average memory access time:
where T_P is physical-memory access time and T_D is disk access time. The disk gap is so large that even tiny miss rates dominate AMAT.
A desirable property of a page-replacement policy is the stack property: increasing the cache size never makes the miss rate worse. Surprisingly, not every reasonable-looking policy has it.

FIFO eviction violates the stack property — on certain access sequences, growing the cache strictly increases the number of misses. This is Belady's anomaly. It is what motivates LRU and the clock algorithm-style policies, which do have the stack property because they only depend on relative access recency.

The optimal eviction policy (offline) is to evict the page whose next use is farthest in the future. Unimplementable in practice (you don't know the future), but useful as a theoretical upper bound to compare against.

Two refinements make eviction practical on real workloads. A pure global policy lets one greedy process hog memory, which Segmented FIFO fixes with a per-process resident-set cap; and writing victims back one page at a time wastes disk bandwidth, which Page clustering amortizes by batching the dirty list.

A process-aware refinement of LRU and the clock algorithm. Pure FIFO is a global policy — it does not distinguish memory by owner, so one greedy process can evict everyone else's pages. Segmented FIFO instead maintains a per-process FIFO queue capped at that process's resident set size, bounding how much physical memory any single process can hold.
Evicted pages are not discarded outright; they move to one of two secondary lists:

html:ol.html:li: Clean page free list — evicted pages that were never modified.
Dirty page list — evicted pages that must be written back before reuse.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The secondary lists act as a victim cache: if a process faults on a page still sitting on the dirty list, it can be reclaimed without going to disk. The resident-set cap gives fairness; the secondary lists soften the cost of capping it too aggressively.

An optimization where two address spaces share the same physical page until one writes to it. Both processes have PTEs pointing at the same PPN, but with the write bit cleared. The first write traps to the OS, which allocates a fresh page, copies the contents, points the writing process's PTE at the new page (with writes re-enabled), and resumes.
Used pervasively:

html:ul.html:li: fork() — the child shares the parent's pages until either modifies one. Avoids the cost of copying an entire address space the child often immediately overwrites with exec().
Shared libraries — many processes map the same library text pages.
Kernel mapped into every process — same physical kernel code seen from every address space.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When a process asks for new heap memory, the OS must guarantee the returned pages contain zeros (to avoid leaking other processes' data). The naive implementation zeroes the page at allocation time. On-demand zeroing defers that work to first touch: until the process actually accesses a freshly-allocated page, no zero-fill happens.

The OS marks the page as not-present in the Page table. The first access traps as a Page fault, the handler zeroes a physical page, installs it in the PTE, and resumes. Heap memory that is allocated but never used costs nothing.

An amortization trick for the write side of page replacement. Writing one dirty page to swap at a time pays a full disk I/O per eviction, and small random writes are the worst case for a disk. Page clustering batches them: the OS collects a group of dirty pages from the global dirty list and writes them out together in a single larger, more sequential I/O. The per-page cost of paging out falls because the fixed overhead of a disk write is spread across the whole batch.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A common convention is to map the kernel into every process's address space (via Copy-on-write) so kernel code and structures appear at the same virtual addresses everywhere.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Replaces the calling Process's program with a new executable. exec() does not return on success — the original code, data, stack, and heap are discarded and the new program runs in their place. The PID is unchanged.
What exec() does internally:

html:ol.html:li: Loads the new executable's code and static data, overwriting the current.
Re-initializes stack and heap.
Jumps to the new program's entry point with arguments passed as argv.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The canonical Unix pattern is fork() then exec() — the shell uses this to launch a command without becoming that command:
Redirection is implemented by opening a file in the child between fork() and exec(), attached to STDOUT_FILENO:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The many variants — execl, execle, execlp, execv, execvp, execvP — differ in how they take arguments (list vs. vector), whether they use the PATH environment variable, and whether they take an explicit environment.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The Unix system call that duplicates the calling Process. After fork() returns, two processes exist where there was one. The child gets its own copy of:

html:ol.html:li: Address space.
General-purpose registers.
Program counter — the child returns from fork() at the same instruction the parent does.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The two processes are distinguished by fork()'s return value:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Which process is scheduled first after fork() is non-deterministic. Use wait() if you need ordering.
Remark. The copy is logical, not physical: real implementations use Copy-on-write so the duplicated address space only allocates fresh pages when one side writes to them.
